coredata.dc:title: Preparation and visible light photocatalytic activity of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanosheet composites
coredata.prism:copyright: Crown copyright © 2013 Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Xuejiao
Liu, Jianjun
Yu, Yingchun
Zuo, Shengli
Li, Baoshan
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (C QDs)/TiO2 nanosheet (TNS) composites were prepared by a simple low temperature process in which TNS were dispersed in C QDs solution, and dried at 60°C. The C QDs/TNS composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM. The results indicated that C QDs were well combined with TNS through surface carbon–oxygen groups. The photocatalytic activity was investigated by degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic activity of C QDs/TNS composites was significantly enhanced compared with that of C QDs/P25 composites and pure TNS, which indicated that the unique up-converted photoluminescence behavior of C QDs and highly reactive {001} facets of TNS both played important roles in the enhancement of photocatalytic activity of C QDs/TNS composites.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of C QDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Up-converted PL spectra of C QDs with excitation of visible-near infrared wavelengths. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XRD patterns of C QDs/TNS composites with different amount of C QDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Raman spectra of pristine graphite, TNS and C QDs/TNS composites. Inset shows magnified TNS and C QDs/TNS composites spectra ranging from 1100 to 2000cm−1. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS spectra of C QDs/TNS composites for full survey, C 1s, Ti2p and O 1s spectra. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
FT-IR spectra of C QDs, TNS and C QDs/TNS composites. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
HRTEM image of C QDs/TNS composites. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
UV–visible absorption spectra of C QDs/TNS composites with different amounts of C QDs. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photocatalytic degradation of RhB solution by C QDs/TNS composites (a) and C QDs/P25 composites (b) with different amount of C QDs under visible light irradiation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Transient photocurrent response curves of TNS, C QDs/TNS and C QDs/P25 composites. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic illustration for the photocatalytic process of C QDs/TNS composites under visible light irradiation. (A color version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Preparation and visible light photocatalytic activity of carbon quantum dots/TiO2 nanosheet composites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (C QDs)/TiO2 nanosheet (TNS) composites were prepared by a simple low temperature process in which TNS were dispersed in C QDs solution, and dried at 60°C. The C QDs/TNS composites were characterized by X-ray diffraction, Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM. The results indicated that C QDs were well combined with TNS through surface carbon–oxygen groups. The photocatalytic activity was investigated by degradation of rhodamine B under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic activity of C QDs/TNS composites was significantly enhanced compared with that of C QDs/P25 composites and pure TNS, which indicated that the unique up-converted photoluminescence behavior of C QDs and highly reactive {001} facets of TNS both played important roles in the enhancement of photocatalytic activity of C QDs/TNS composites.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs), usually consist of group II–VI, III–V compounds or IV elements with a diameter of 1–10nm, have attracted considerable interests [1], especially group IV of C QDs. The first preparation of C QDs has been reported through electrophoresis in 2004 [2], since then, many other synthetic methods including electrochemical synthesis [3,4], hydrothermal synthesis [5,6], acidic oxidation [7,8], microwave [9], laser ablation [10] etc. have been studied. Compared with traditional group II–VI, III–V QDs, C QDs possess many good characteristics, such as high aqueous solubility, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, strong size effect, unique up-converted photoluminescence (PL) behavior and photo-induced electron transfer property, which indicate that C QDs can act as an efficient component in design of photocatalyst.

Many studies for synthesis of C QDs/semiconductor composites and their enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity have been reported. C QDs/Fe2O3 composites synthesized through one-step hydrothermal method with enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity for degrading gaseous benzene and methanol have been reported by Zhang et al. [11] firstly. Then, Yu et al. [12] have reported a solvothermal fabrication of C QDs/Fe2O3 composites for degrading methylene blue (MB) under visible light irradiation. C QDs/ZnO composites prepared by one-step hydrothermal reaction of zinc-containing salts and carbon-containing sources including l-ascorbic acid, glucose and electrolytic carbon, have shown good photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation [13–15]. Among the known semiconductors, TiO2 displays outstanding properties as a photocatalyst, due to its good chemical stability, photocatalytic activity and environmental friendly behavior, etc [16]. But TiO2 has a relatively large band gap of 3.0–3.2eV and cannot be excited by visible light. Previous studies have found that C QDs can enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 nanoparticle (TNP) under visible light irradiation [3]. Zhuo et al. [17] have found an ultrasonic route for fabrication of graphene QDs and prepared graphene QDs/TNP composites for degradation of MB. Hai et al. [18] have reported a fabrication of C QDs/TNP composites through hydrothermal method and studied the visible light photocatalytic activity of C QDs/TNP composites.

TNP is exposed mainly with {101} facets, previous theoretical and experimental studies indicated that {001} facets are much more reactive than {101} facets. TiO2 nanosheet (TNS), a new two-dimensional nanomaterial with a large percentage of {001} facets, has attracted more attention recently. It has been confirmed that TNS with highly reactive {001} facets exhibits enhanced photocatalytic activity than that of TNP under ultraviolet irradiation [19,20]. However, pure TNS only exhibits photocatalytic activity under ultraviolet irradiation. Recent studies have found that doping TNS with nonmetal elements such as nitrogen [21] and carbon [22] etc. can enhance the photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation. Nevertheless, to the best of our knowledge, few studies have been reported on the fabrication of composites consisting of C QDs and TNS, and their application in photocatalysis under visible light irradiation.

In this paper, we present a simple method for fabrication of C QDs/TNS composites with superior photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation. The important roles of C QDs and structure of TiO2 in C QDs/TNS composites in the enhancement of photocatalytic activity are discussed.

All the reagents were analytical grade and used without further purification. TNS were prepared by hydrothermal method [23]. C QDs were synthesized through an alkali-assisted electro-chemical method [3]. In the experiment, 15mg C QDs was obtained and dissolved in 20mL ethanol, the concentration of C QDs solution was 0.0625mol/L.

C QDs/TNS composites were synthesized by a simple low temperature process in which 0.1g TNS were dispersed in 10mL C QDs solution, and dried at 60°C overnight. C QDs/TNS composites prepared by changing the amount of C QDs solution of 2.5, 5.0, 10 and 20mL were labeled as C 2.5/TNS, C 5/TNS, C 10/TNS and C 20/TNS, respectively. In addition, the C QDs/P25 composites with different amount of C QDs were synthesized through the same procedure.

PL spectra were recorded on a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer with photomultiplier tube voltage of 400V and scanning speed of 1200nm/min. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) measurements were conducted on Hitachi 800 and JEOL-2010 microscope, respectively. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were measured by an ESCALAB-250 with monochromated Al Kα radiation. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10 IR spectrophotometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a Bruker D8FOCUS powder diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectra were recorded on a Renishaw inVia Raman spectrometer with a 785nm laser beam. UV–visible absorption spectra were collected on a TU-1901 spectrophotometer (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd.). Surface area data were determined by nitrogen adsorption/desorption, using a Quantachrome Autosorb-1 apparatus. The photocurrent experiments were performed on a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, China) in a standard three-electrode system with Pt wire and Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) electrode as counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. A Xe lamp with a cutoff filter (λ >420nm) at intensity of 100mW/cm2 was used for visible light irradiation. 0.5M Na2SO4 aqueous solution was used as the supporting electrolyte. The working electrode was prepared as follows: ground 0.2g C QDs/TNS or C QDs/P25 composites power with 0.05g graphite powder and suitable amount of polytetrafluoroethylene emulsion (60wt.%), and rolled them into a thin slice with the thickness about 150μm. Then, cut the thin slice into 1×2cm and pressed it on nickel foam (thickness: 1.0mm, pore density: 110ppi, from Shenzhen Rolinsia Power Materials Ltd.).

The photocatalytic activities of C QDs/TNS composites were evaluated by degradation of rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light irradiation. A 500W Xe lamp with a cutoff filter (λ >420nm) was used for visible light irradiation. The light source was positioned above 15cm from the surface of reaction solution. 0.1g C QDs/TNS composites were suspended in 100mL, 10ppm RhB aqueous solution and stirred for 30min to ensure the establishment of an adsorption/desorption equilibrium between RhB and photocatalyst before irradiation. Under stirring, a small amount of suspension (about 5mL) was taken out every 20min under irradiation and centrifuged at 5000rpm for 10min, then analyzed using a 752 spectrophotometer (Shandong Lunan Ruihong Co., Ltd.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of C QDs
ce:section.ce:para: HRTEM image of Fig. S1 shows that the obtained C QDs are spherical with a diameter of 5nm, the lattice spacing of 0.321nm is in good agreement with the (002) plane of graphitic carbon. In the electrolysis process, two graphite electrodes were used as both anode and cathode. A large number of bubbles from cathode graphite and simultaneously no obvious change of anode graphite were observed during the electrolysis. The electrolyte turned from colorless to yellow and the anode graphite became corrosive at the end of the electrolysis. It was found that the C QDs were synthesized based on the anodic oxidation of graphite, as shown in Fig. S2. Firstly, hydroxyl and oxygen radicals (OH and O−) are produced due to anodic oxidation of water. Secondly, anode graphite is attacked by these highly active radicals, then, some small graphite particles exfoliate from edge sites, grain boundaries, or defect sites gradually and dissolve in the mixture of ethanol and water to form C QDs solution [24]. According to the mechanism, surfaces of the exfoliated C QDs should be decorated by some oxygen groups, which was confirmed by the following FT-IR spectrum.

The up-converted PL spectra of C QDs measured by using excitation wavelengths ranging from 600 to 900nm with the interval of 50nm are shown in Fig. 1 . The up-converted emission exhibits an excitation-dependent behavior and the emission wavelengths are located in the range from 330 to 530nm. As we known, anatase TiO2 with broad energy gap of 3.2eV only can be excited by the ultraviolet light with wavelength shorter than 380nm. The up-converted PL property of C QDs suggested that C QDs can be used as an efficient energy transfer material to convert visible light and near-infrared light to shorter wavelengths for exciting TiO2.

As shown in Fig. 2 , all C QDs/TNS composites and TNS exhibit the same characteristic diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2 (JCPDS card No. 21-1272). No characteristic diffraction peak of carbon is detected for C QDs/TNS composites, which is explained by the small amounts, high dispersion and low crystallinity of C QDs in C QDs/TNS composites.

As shown in Fig. 3 , four strong peaks at 140, 397, 517 and 643cm−1 are clearly observed in both TNS and C QDs/TNS composites, which correspond to the E g(1), B 1g(1), A 1g + B 1g(2) and E g(3) modes of anatase TiO2, respectively. Besides, two additional peaks at 1430 and 1610cm−1 are observed for C QDs/TNS composites in inset of Fig. 3, which can be attributed to the D and G band of carbon, respectively. The result indicates the existence of C QDs in the C QDs/TNS composites. In addition, the D band is related to the presence of sp3 defects, the G band is related to the in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon atoms, and the intensity ratio of D band to G band (I D/I G) shows the graphitization degree of carbon [25]. In our case, the pristine graphite has a low I D/I G of 0.15, but the I D/I G increases to 1.30 for C QDs/TNS composites, which indicates the increase of defects and lattice disorders of C QDs in C QDs/TNS composites. This increase of I D/I G ratio can be ascribed to the successful combination of C QDs and TNS [26].

XPS was used to study the components and surface properties of the C QDs/TNS composites. The full survey spectrum of Fig. 4 indicates the presence of titanium (Ti 2p), carbon (C 1s), and oxygen (O 1s) in the C QDs/TNS composites. The Ti 2p spectrum fitted to two peaks at 458.9 and 464.4eV are corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of TiO2, respectively. The main peak of C 1s spectrum at 284.8eV is attributed to the C–C bond with sp2 orbital. The peak at 286.3 and 287.3eV are assigned to the C–O and CO bonds, respectively. No peak of Ti–C bond is found in both Ti 2p and C 1s spectra, which indicates that C QDs do not exist as a dopant in TNS. The peaks at 530.0, 530.7 and 532.1eV for O 1s spectrum are ascribed to TiO, CO and C–OH. Both the C 1s and O 1s spectra indicate the existence of carbon–oxygen bonds in C QDs/TNS composites.

In addition, the atomic concentrations of carbon determined by XPS for TNS, C 2.5/TNS, C 5/TNS, C 10/TNS and C 20/TNS are 12.37%, 14.46%, 25.39%, 30.21% and 34.26%, respectively. The atomic concentration of carbon for pure TNS is as high as 12.37%, because XPS analysis is easily affected by adventitious carbon species. However, the atomic concentrations of carbon increase with increasing the amount of C QDs in C QDs/TNS composites, which means the relative amounts of C QDs in C QDs/TNS composites increase.

The FT-IR spectra are shown in Fig. 5 , peaks at 3420 and 1629cm−1 appeared in both TNS and C QDs/TNS composites are attributed to vibrations of surface adsorbed water. The peak at 1400cm−1 of C–O stretching vibration for C QDs has shifted to 1455cm−1 for C QDs/TNS composites and a new peak at 1371cm−1 of epoxy C–O stretching vibration has appeared in C QDs/TNS composites. The shift to higher wavenumber and the appearance of new C–O peaks in C QDs/TNS composites can be explained by the successfully interactions between TNS and C QDs [27]. The result of FT-IR measurement, together with the result of XPS measurement, suggest that C QDs/TNS composites are formed through C–O bond arisen from the surfaces of C QDs and TNS.

Fig. 6 shows the HRTEM image of C QDs/TNS composites. It can be observed that C QDs are attached to the surfaces of TNS. The TEM and HRTEM images of Fig. S3 show that the as-prepared TNS consist of well-defined rectangular anatase TiO2 nanosheets. The lattice spacing of 0.350 and 0.235nm correspond to the (101) and (001) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively. The average side size and thickness of TNS are about 45–55 and 6nm, respectively. The percentage of exposed {001} facets was calculated from the equation of S {0
0
1}/(S {0
0
1}+S {1
0
1})= b 2/[b 2+(a+b)(a−b)tag(68.3°)] [20]. Where, S is the area of crystal plane, a and b are the average side sizes as shown in inset of Fig. S3a and measured from 50 particles. The calculated percentage of exposed {001} facets on the TNS is about 68%.

As shown in Fig. 7 , TNS exhibits no absorption in the visible region of 400–800nm, which accords with the intrinsic absorption property of TiO2. While all the C QDs/TNS composites exhibit obvious absorption in visible region, the absorption intensity slightly increases with increasing the amount of C QDs in the composites. This increasing of absorption intensity can be ascribed to the addition of C QDs.

Before photocatalytic study under visible light irradiation, the adsorption property of C 10/TNS composites was studied in dark. The adsorption/desorption equilibrium is almost established in 30min as shown in Fig. S4. According to this result, reaction solution was stirred for 30min to ensure the establishment of an adsorption/desorption equilibrium before visible light irradiation.

As shown in Fig. 8 , there is no photocatalytic degradation of RhB in the presense of only pure TNS, P25 or C QDs, while C QDs/TNS and C QDs/P25 composites exhibit enhanced photocatalytic activities. Especially C QDs/TNS composites, exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity than C QDs/P25 composites. Moreover, with increasing the amount of as-prepared C QDs solution from 2.5 to 10mL, the degradation efficiency increases gradually from 27.2% to 95.4%, which indicates that introducing a suitable amount of C QDs can effectively improve the visible photocatalytic activity of TNS for the degradation of RhB. Further increasing the amount of C QDs leads an obvious decrease of degradation efficiency. Because graphite can absorb visible light [28], higher content of C QDs in the C QDs/TNS composites can result in a competition for visible light absorption, the availability of visible light for degradation RhB is decreased.

In addition, the surface area for TNS, C 2.5/TNS, C 5/TNS, C 10/TNS and C 20/TNS are 107.6, 82.743, 71.799, 39.8 and 29.788m2 g−1, respectively. The surface area significantly decreases with increasing the amount of C QDs, because the added C QDs can block the stacking pores in TNS. Meanwhile, this decrease of surface area indicates that surface area plays minor role in affecting photocatalytic activity of C QDs/TNS composites.

Based on the above results, we know that introducing C QDs to TiO2 can significantly improve the photocatalytic activity of pure TiO2 under visible light irradiation. Moreover, C QDs/TNS composites exhibit much higher photocatalytic activity than C QDs/P25 composites. Therefore, we conclude that the enhanced photocatalytic activity benefits from both the introduction of C QDs and phase structures of TiO2.

On the one hand, it is well known that P25 consisting of anatase (80%) and rutile (20%) TiO2, is dominated by {101} facets of anatase TiO2, but TNS with 68% of exposed {001} facets, is dominated by more reactive {001} facets. More titanium atoms and oxygen deficiencies are exposed on the {001} facets because the Ti atoms are five-coordinated on {001} facets, thus more dioxygen-derived oxygen atoms are adsorpted on the {001} facets when TNS are excited. This behavior can promote the transfer of interfacial electrons and separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs for TNS [29]. Therefore, C QDs/TNS composites can exhibit higher photocatalytic activity than C QDs/P25 composites.

In order to investigate the transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons of C QDs/TNS and C QDs/P25 composites, transient photocurrent response curves were measured. As shown in Fig. 9 , both C QDs/TNS and C QDs/P25 composites exhibit fast and reproducible transient photocurrent responses under several on–off cycles of intermittent visible light irradiation. It is notable that C QDs/TNS composites exhibit stronger transient photocurrent response which is about 3 times larger than that of C QDs/P25 composites. The result indicates that C QDs/TNS composites possess higher transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons and separation efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole pairs than C QDs/P25 composites.

On the other hand, the roles of C QDs for the enhancement of photocatalytic activity of C QDs/TNS composites are illustrated in Fig. 10 . Firstly, C QDs can absorb visible light and near-infrared light and then convert them to shorter wavelengths due to the up-converted property [30]. The shorter wavelengths that less than or equal to 380nm can excite TNS to produce electron–hole pairs. Secondly, C QDs can accept the photogenerated electrons from TNS and promote the separation of photogenerated electron–hole pairs [30], which is proved by transient photocurrent response and PL measurement. The introduction of C QDs to TNS has significantly increased its transient photocurrent response and decreased its PL intensity, as shown in Fig. 9 and Fig. S5, respectively. The aboved two results indicate that C QDs can help for the transfer of photogenerated electrons, and then reduce the recombination rate of electron–hole pairs [31]. So, redundant electrons can activate the adsorbed oxygen (O2) on the surface of composites to produce superoxide radical anions (O2 −), and the holes can react with water to form active hydroxy radicals (OH), which subsequently degraded the RhB solution [12].

We have demonstrated the preparation of a new photocatalyst (C QDs/TNS composites) through a simple low temperature process. It was found that TNS with 68% of exposed {001} facets, was combined with C QDs through surface carbon–oxygen groups, and the photocatalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for RhB degradation under visible light irradiation. However, pure TNS and C QDs/P25 composites exhibited lower photocatalytic activity, which indicated that both the C QDs and phase structure of TiO2 played important roles for the high photocatalytic activity of C QDs/TNS composites.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 10972025.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2013.11.053.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This document file contains supplementary figures.
